hero.tagline: Massachusetts small claims courts handle disputes up to $7,000, with exceptions for certain cases like property damage from automobile accidents.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Exceptions apply for certain cases like property damage from automobile accidents.

at_a_glance.label: Court
at_a_glance.value: District Court, Boston Municipal Court, or Housing Court
at_a_glance.detail: Depending on the case type and location.

at_a_glance.label: Lawyers at trial
at_a_glance.detail: Parties may represent themselves or hire an attorney.

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Defendant may appeal for a new trial.

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years.

limits.split_claims_explanation: If a claim exceeds the $7,000 limit, you may waive the excess and file in small claims court, or file the entire claim in a higher court. Splitting one dispute into multiple claims to stay within the limit is not allowed.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Massachusetts allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed up to $7,000. The statute of limitations varies depending on the type of contract.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend someone $1,000, and they failed to repay.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring any evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a friend $2,500 with the understanding they'd repay in six months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the loan agreement and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $5,000 and failed to make payments as agreed.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided consulting services worth $3,000, but the client hasn't paid the invoice.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $1,200 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $2,000 in unpaid rent for the past two months.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement, rent ledger, and any communication with the tenant.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for goods with a $500 check that bounced.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from the date of the bad check. Provide the bounced check and any bank notices.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $4,000 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted, leaving you responsible.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the loan agreement and records of payments made on behalf of the borrower.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Massachusetts allows consumers to file claims up to $7,000 for issues like defective products or poor services. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Defective product purchase
claims.example: You bought a $1,500 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from date of purchase. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service
claims.example: You paid $2,000 for home cleaning services that were not performed as agreed.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement, payment proof, and evidence of poor service.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $3,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices, payment proof, and any expert opinions.

claims.name: Incomplete or poor-quality contractor work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $6,000 for a renovation that was left unfinished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You paid $1,000 for a DJ who never showed up at your event.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from service date. Bring the service agreement, payment proof, and any communication.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges
claims.example: A company continued to charge your credit card $50 monthly after you canceled the subscription.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from discovery. Provide cancellation proof and billing statements.
claims.damage_boost: Potential for double or triple damages under consumer protection laws.

claims.name: Defective used car purchase
claims.example: You bought a used car for $6,500 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Bring the sales contract, warranty, and repair records.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product for $200 that didn't perform as promised.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from discovery. Provide the advertisement, purchase receipt, and evidence of misrepresentation.
claims.damage_boost: Potential for double or triple damages under consumer protection laws.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Massachusetts allows claims up to $7,000 for property damage, with exceptions for automobile accidents where higher amounts may be claimed.

claims.name: Vehicle collision property damage
claims.example: Another driver caused $8,000 in damage to your car.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from accident date. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and photos of damage.

claims.name: Neighbor caused damage to your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell and damaged your fence, causing $2,500 in repairs.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left $5,000 worth of damage upon moving out.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Bring the lease agreement, move-in/move-out inspection reports, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Pet caused damage to property
claims.example: A neighbor's dog chewed through your $1,200 patio furniture.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left a $3,000 watch for repair, and it was returned damaged.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Bring the service agreement, photos of damage, and any communication with the custodian.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Massachusetts allows tenants and landlords to file claims up to $7,000 for monetary disputes. Eviction cases are handled separately.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $2,000 security deposit without justification.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.
claims.damage_boost: Potential for triple damages if the withholding was willful.

claims.name: Failure to provide itemized list for deposit deductions
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $1,500 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement and any communication regarding the deposit.
claims.damage_boost: Potential for triple damages if the withholding was willful.

claims.name: Uninhabitable rental conditions
claims.example: Your apartment had no heat during winter, and the landlord refused to fix it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Provide photos, repair requests, and any health department reports.

claims.name: Landlord retained tenant's property after move-out
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord kept your $1,000 bicycle left behind by mistake.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Bring the lease agreement and any communication regarding the property.

claims.name: Landlord conducted illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from eviction date. Provide the lease agreement and any communication regarding the eviction.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but the landlord didn't return the last month's rent after early termination.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement and payment records.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,000 in unpaid rent over three months.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement and rent ledger.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted cost from rent
claims.example: You paid $500 to fix a leaking roof after the landlord refused, and deducted it from rent.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from repair date. Provide repair receipts and communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wages
claim_categories.blurb: Massachusetts allows employees to file claims up to $7,000 for unpaid wages and related issues. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 in unpaid wages for hours worked.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from wage due date. Bring pay stubs and any communication regarding unpaid wages.
claims.damage_boost: Potential for treble damages and attorney's fees.

claims.name: Employer failed to provide final paycheck
claims.example: After termination, your employer didn't give you your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from termination date. Provide termination notice and any communication regarding the final paycheck.
claims.damage_boost: Potential for treble damages and attorney's fees.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions that your employer hasn't paid.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from commission due date. Bring commission agreements and sales records.
claims.damage_boost: Potential for treble damages and attorney's fees.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but weren't paid the additional $500 owed.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from overtime worked. Provide timesheets and pay stubs.
claims.damage_boost: Potential for treble damages and attorney's fees.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Massachusetts allows individuals to file claims up to $7,000 for personal disputes. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a neighbor's icy walkway and incurred $2,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from injury date. Bring medical records and any communication with the property owner.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,000 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from demand for return. Provide evidence of the loan and any communication regarding the return.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: Someone stole your $500 bicycle from your yard.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from theft date. Provide police reports and any evidence of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Massachusetts allows claims up to $7,000 for vehicle-related disputes, with exceptions for certain cases like property damage from automobile accidents.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices and any communication with the mechanic.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and you incurred $300 in fees.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from tow date. Provide towing receipts and photos of the parking area.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction cases are not handled in small claims court and must be filed in the appropriate housing or district court.
Defamation cases are excluded from small claims court due to their complexity and the need for formal procedures.
Professional malpractice claims require expert testimony and are not suitable for small claims court.
Divorce, child custody, and support cases are handled by family courts, not small claims court.
Claims against federal agencies must be filed in federal court, not state small claims court.
Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Small claims court cannot issue orders requiring someone to do or stop doing something.
These claims are handled by the Department of Industrial Accidents, not small claims court.
Issues related to wills and estates are handled by probate courts, not small claims court.
Disputes over property ownership are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are generally not awarded in small claims court. However, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases, such as consumer protection violations.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Under the Massachusetts Consumer Protection Act, a court may award up to triple damages for willful or knowing violations.
The holder of a bad check may recover damages equal to twice the amount of the check, plus attorney's fees and costs.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney's fees may be awarded in cases involving consumer protection violations or bad checks, as specified by statute.
damages.postjudgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at 12% per annum from the date of judgment until paid.

where_to_file.court_name: District Court, Boston Municipal Court, or Housing Court

filing_options: Court where either the plaintiff or defendant lives or has a usual place of business or employment
Housing Court where the property is located

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: If filed in the wrong venue, the case may be transferred to the correct court, potentially causing delays.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing is available through the Massachusetts Guide and File program for small claims cases.

service_fees.notes: The court sends notice to the defendant via certified mail at no additional cost.
If certified mail fails, personal service by sheriff or constable may be required at an additional cost.

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (e.g., SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% of the federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and service costs.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are added to the judgment against the defendant.

other_fees.name: Appeal filing
other_fees.notes: Fee for filing an appeal in small claims court.

other_fees.name: Writ of execution
other_fees.notes: Fee for issuing a writ of execution to enforce a judgment.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment against the defendant.

entries.notes: Applies to contracts for the sale of goods.
Applies to written promises to pay money.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

entries.notes: Applies to damage to personal or real property.
Discovery rule applies; clock starts when fraud is discovered.
Applies to claims for unpaid compensation.
Applies to recovery of security deposits.
Applies to actions to enforce court judgments.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the statute of limitations when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can facilitate settlement and is advisable before filing a claim.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: A 30-day written notice is required before pursuing damages for a bad check.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: A written demand for unpaid wages is required before filing a claim, and failure to pay within 30 days may result in treble damages.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days of lease termination when withholding a security deposit.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: A 30-day demand letter is required before filing a claim under the Consumer Protection Act.

forms.name: Statement of Small Claim and Notice of Trial
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case and notifies the defendant of the trial date.

forms.name: Affidavit of Indigency
forms.description: Requests a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Application for Appeal
forms.description: Filed by the defendant to appeal a small claims judgment.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: The court clerk sends the Statement of Small Claim and Notice of Trial to the defendant via certified mail.
cons: May fail if defendant doesn't accept mail

methods.name: Personal service by sheriff or constable
methods.description: A sheriff or constable personally delivers the court documents to the defendant.
cons: May be required if mail service fails

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation. For sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with the laws of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, the court may allow service by publication.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, the court may permit alternative methods of service.

response_and_default.response_notes: The defendant is not required to file a written answer but may do so. They must appear at the scheduled hearing.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal within 10 days.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may represent themselves or be represented by an attorney.
hearing.format: Informal bench trial, typically lasting 10-15 minutes per case.
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter from the court clerk in advance of the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks.
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Mediation may be offered on the day of trial to help parties reach a settlement.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal results in a new trial before a judge or jury.
appeals.bond_amount_notes: A $100 bond is required unless waived by the court.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: The defendant may appeal a default judgment within 10 days.
appeals.frivolous_appeal_notes: The court may award additional costs up to $100 for frivolous appeals.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at 12% per annum from the date of judgment until paid.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of execution with the court, serve it on the debtor's employer, and the employer will withhold a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.
exemptions: 75% of disposable earnings or 50 times the federal minimum hourly wage per week, whichever is greater.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution, serve it on the debtor's bank, and the bank will freeze and turn over funds to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the bank account.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: Record the judgment with the county registry of deeds to place a lien on the debtor's property, which must be satisfied before the property can be sold or refinanced.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Authorize a sheriff to seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt property.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, and the sheriff will seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has valuable non-exempt property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: Judgments are valid for 20 years and cannot be renewed.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge the judgment, depending on the type of debt.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are recorded.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses may represent themselves in small claims court without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a fictitious name must file a business certificate with the city or town clerk.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: A sole proprietor may authorize an employee to represent the business in court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from recovering fees for services requiring a license.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: If you sue the wrong party or use incorrect information, the case may be dismissed or the judgment unenforceable.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the exact legal name and address of the defendant before filing. For businesses, check with the Secretary of State's office.

pitfalls.title: Missing statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will result in dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Determine the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within that period.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Without adequate evidence, you may lose your case even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements to support your claim.

pitfalls.title: Failure to appear in court
pitfalls.what_happens: If you don't show up for your hearing, your case may be dismissed or you could lose by default.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Mark your calendar and ensure you attend the scheduled hearing.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring court procedures
pitfalls.what_happens: Not following court rules can lead to delays or dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with the court's procedures and comply with all requirements.

pitfalls.title: Overestimating claim amount
pitfalls.what_happens: Claiming more than the allowable limit can result in dismissal or transfer to a higher court.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure your claim does not exceed the $7,000 limit, or waive the excess to stay within small claims jurisdiction.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in small claims court. The process is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves. However, you may choose to hire an attorney if you prefer.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The timeline varies, but most small claims cases are scheduled for a hearing within 30 to 70 days after filing. The exact duration depends on the court's schedule and the specifics of your case.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: In Massachusetts, the maximum amount you can sue for in small claims court is $7,000. Exceptions apply for certain cases, such as property damage from automobile accidents, where higher amounts may be claimed.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear at the scheduled hearing, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor. You will still need to provide evidence to support your claim before the judgment is granted.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: In Massachusetts, only the defendant has the right to appeal a small claims judgment. The appeal must be filed within 10 days of the judgment and results in a new trial before a judge or jury.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Massachusetts is valid for 20 years. During this time, you can take steps to collect the judgment, such as wage garnishment or placing a lien on the debtor's property.

state_specific_notes: Massachusetts allows claims up to $7,000 in small claims court, with exceptions for certain cases like property damage from automobile accidents.
The defendant has the right to appeal a small claims judgment within 10 days, resulting in a new trial.
Prejudgment and postjudgment interest rates are set at 12% per annum.
Attorney's fees may be awarded in cases involving consumer protection violations or bad checks.
E-filing is available through the Massachusetts Guide and File program for small claims cases.

interesting_findings: Massachusetts small claims court allows for statutory multipliers in certain cases, such as up to triple damages for consumer protection violations.
The court may offer on-site mediation on the day of trial to help parties reach a settlement.
Businesses may represent themselves in small claims court without an attorney, and can authorize an employee to appear on their behalf.

sources.label: Massachusetts Court System — Small Claims Information
sources.citation: Massachusetts Court System — Small Claims Information

Illegal Lockout — Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 186, § 14
